text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP OFFICE VISIT
Attending: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), germline BRCA2-mutated, presenting for follow-up approximately 4 months after initiation of olaparib (Lynparza). Visit is occurring approximately 3 weeks after hospital-based packed red blood cell (pRBC) transfusion and dose reduction of olaparib from 300 mg BID to 200 mg BID following Grade 3 anemia. Today's visit focuses on reassessment of tolerability, CBC interval labs, and ongoing disease response.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is a retired steelworker with a well-characterized history of high-risk metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer diagnosed approximately 3 years ago, at that time presenting with a PSA of 187 ng/mL, Gleason 9 (4+5) adenocarcinoma, and metastatic disease involving the lumbar spine (L3, L4), right iliac wing, right femoral neck, and a right obturator lymph node. He harbors a germline BRCA2 pathogenic frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome — a finding that has now directly informed his current therapeutic strategy.

He was initially managed with androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) using leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone, with an excellent initial PSA response to nadir of 0.8 ng/mL. He progressed biochemically and radiographically approximately 24 months later (CRPC designation), at which point abiraterone was discontinued and he was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily. He achieved a PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL on enzalutamide, with stable disease on bone scan and nodal response noted on CT. However, approximately 6 months into enzalutamide, he again progressed — PSA rose to 8.3 ng/mL, imaging on PSMA PET/CT confirmed marked osseous progression with multiple new bone metastases throughout the thoracic and lumbar spine, bilateral iliac crests, ribs, sternum, and a growing right obturator lymph node — and enzalutamide was discontinued.

Given his confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss (germline mutation plus somatic second hit with LOH confirmed on prior biopsy, and confirmed again on CT-guided bone biopsy of the right iliac crest immediately prior to this line of therapy), olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID was initiated approximately 4 months ago per FDA-approved indication in BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC. Leuprolide was continued for ongoing castrate testosterone suppression. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks was continued for bone protection.

At his 6-week follow-up on olaparib, PSA had declined to 4.1 ng/mL — a 51% reduction from baseline — consistent with a meaningful early biochemical response. At that time, Hgb had already begun to fall to 10.2 g/dL. He was managed with watchful waiting and instructed to monitor for symptoms.

Approximately 3 months into olaparib, he developed symptomatic Grade 3 anemia with Hgb of 7.8 g/dL, presenting with dyspnea on exertion and worsening fatigue. Reticulocyte count was low at 1.1%, consistent with hypoproliferative anemia from olaparib-related myelosuppression. Contributing factors included borderline iron deficiency (ferritin 68 ng/mL, TIBC 320, serum iron 45). B12 and folate were normal. Hemolysis workup was negative. He received 2 units of pRBCs with post-transfusion Hgb rise to 10.1 g/dL. IV iron was administered as ferric carboxymaltose 750 mg. Olaparib was dose-reduced to 200 mg BID per FDA labeling guidance for hematologic toxicity. He was not started on an ESA given the oncologic context and thromboembolic risk considerations.

He presents today for assessment of anemia trajectory, tolerability of olaparib at the reduced dose, and overall disease status.

He reports that since the dose reduction and transfusion approximately 3 weeks ago, he has felt "considerably better." Fatigue has improved significantly — he rates it as mild (Grade 1) and is now able to manage light household activities and walk around the neighborhood. Nausea, which had been a persistent Grade 1 complaint on the higher dose, has since resolved. He is tolerating oral intake without difficulty. He denies dyspnea at rest or with minimal exertion. He reports no chest pain, palpitations, or lightheadedness.

Musculoskeletal: Bone pain is minimal today. He notes only mild residual aching in the lower back, which he rates 2/10 and manages with acetaminophen 650 mg TID as needed. The right hip discomfort that had been present at the time of CRPC progression has substantially improved. He denies any new focal bone pain or radicular symptoms.

Neurologically, he denies any symptoms suggestive of spinal cord compression — no new weakness in the lower extremities, no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no saddle anesthesia. He did experience a single fall event approximately 2 months into enzalutamide (prior therapy); he has not fallen since being on olaparib.

Current medications reviewed and reconciled (see below). He confirms compliance with olaparib 200 mg BID taken with or without food; he takes it with a small snack to minimize GI upset. He takes calcium carbonate 500 mg BID and vitamin D3 2000 IU daily.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1), improved from Grade 2-3 prior to dose reduction and transfusion. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Mild ongoing unintentional weight loss of approximately 4 lbs over the past 4 months (less than would raise urgent concern at this time but being monitored). Appetite mildly reduced.
Respiratory: Dyspnea on exertion resolved post-transfusion. No cough, hemoptysis, or pleuritis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema. Blood pressure has been well-controlled on home monitoring.
Gastrointestinal: Nausea resolved. No vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation of note. No abdominal pain. No melena or hematochezia.
Genitourinary: Urinary frequency managed; no gross hematuria.
Musculoskeletal: Mild diffuse bone aching, managed with acetaminophen. No new localized pain.
Neurological: No headaches, visual changes, or cognitive decline beyond baseline mild slowing (attributed to prior enzalutamide; resolving). No falls since prior note.
Hematologic: No bruising, petechiae, or spontaneous bleeding.
Endocrine: Hot flashes persist (Grade 1, ADT-related). No adrenal insufficiency symptoms.
Dermatologic: No rash.
Psychiatric: Mildly anxious about disease status but overall coping well with family support. Engaged in goals-of-care discussions.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Olaparib (Lynparza) 200 mg orally BID (dose-reduced from 300 mg BID approximately 3 weeks ago)
2. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (last injection approximately 6 weeks ago, next due in approximately 6 weeks)
3. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks (last dose 3 weeks ago)
4. Prednisone 5 mg orally BID — NOTE: Initially started with abiraterone; the clinical team re-evaluated the necessity of ongoing prednisone following abiraterone discontinuation. At the time enzalutamide was initiated, prednisone was tapered and discontinued. Currently NOT on prednisone.
5. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily (hypertension; dose uptitrated during initial ADT course)
6. Metformin 500 mg orally BID (type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled; HbA1c 6.8% at last check)
7. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime (hyperlipidemia)
8. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally BID (bone protection adjunct)
9. Vitamin D3 2000 IU orally daily
10. Acetaminophen 650 mg orally TID as needed (bone pain)
11. Ondansetron 4 mg orally as needed (nausea — currently not being used regularly)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies. NKDA.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, temperature 98.1°F (36.7°C), oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 174 lbs (down approximately 4 lbs from 178 lbs at prior visit 6 weeks ago), height 5'10", BMI 25.0.

General: Mr. Kowalski is a thin, well-appearing elderly male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and interactive. Appears fatigued but in better spirits than prior visit.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae mildly pale — consistent with mild residual anemia. Oral mucosa moist. No oral ulcers. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi. Adequate air entry.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. No guarding or rigidity. Mild diffuse tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant — not present at baseline, noted today for the first time. No appreciable hepatosplenomegaly on exam today (liver edge not palpable below the costal margin). Bowel sounds present in all quadrants. No CVA tenderness.

Musculoskeletal: No focal bony tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine, sacrum, or right hip today. Mild tenderness with deep palpation over the right posterior iliac crest (site of prior CT-guided biopsy, consistent with residual procedural soreness). Ambulation without assistive device, gait steady.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Reflexes 2+ symmetric. Gait steady with normal heel-to-toe walk. No falls observed.

Skin: No rash, petechiae, ecchymoses, or jaundice. No palmar erythema.

Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy today (previously enlarged right obturator node is deep and not palpable on surface exam).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
Ordered and resulted today (approximately 3 weeks post-transfusion and dose reduction):

Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 9.6 g/dL (down from post-transfusion high of 10.1 g/dL; stable/mildly declining trend; previously 7.8 g/dL at Grade 3 nadir)
- Hematocrit: 29.1%
- MCV: 86 fL (normocytic)
- Platelets: 176 K/uL (within acceptable range; mildly down from 198 K/uL at olaparib start)
- WBC: 4.2 K/uL (mild leukopenia, Grade 1; trending near lower limit)
- ANC: 1.9 K/uL (adequate; no neutropenia meeting dose-reduction criteria at this time)
- Reticulocyte count: 2.8% (improving from 1.1% at nadir — appropriate marrow recovery response post-transfusion and IV iron)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 102 mEq/L, CO2: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 22 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 62 mL/min/1.73m2, stable; mild CKD Grade 2 consistent with age/hypertension)
- Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting; well-controlled DM)
- Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
- AST: 31 U/L, ALT: 27 U/L (within normal limits; reassuring — no hepatic infiltration evident)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 148 U/L (elevated, above normal upper limit of 120 U/L — consistent with ongoing osseous metastatic disease; previously 162 U/L; trending down slightly)
- Albumin: 3.7 g/dL

PSA: 2.8 ng/mL (baseline at olaparib start: 8.3 ng/mL; PSA nadir to date — consistent with continued biochemical response to olaparib despite dose reduction; this is a further meaningful decline from 4.1 ng/mL at 6-week check)

Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL (confirmed castrate levels; ongoing suppression on leuprolide)

LDH: 198 U/L (upper limit of normal; stable from prior)

Iron studies (post-IV iron infusion approximately 3 weeks ago):
- Ferritin: 182 ng/mL (improved from 68 ng/mL; repleted)
- Serum iron: 89 mcg/dL (improved from 45 mcg/dL)
- TIBC: 268 mcg/dL (improved from 320 mcg/dL)
- Transferrin saturation: 33% (repleted)

HbA1c (routine monitoring): 6.9% (stable, well-controlled)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
No new imaging performed at today's visit. Restaging bone scan and CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis are planned for approximately 5 months post-olaparib initiation (i.e., in approximately 4-6 weeks from today). The most recent PSMA PET/CT prior to olaparib initiation showed widespread osseous metastases and an enlarged right obturator lymph node (2.1 cm). Post-transfusion and dose reduction, prior bone scan comparisons at time of enzalutamide response showed sclerotic change at multiple sites. No new imaging to report today.

---

ASSESSMENT
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5) Grade Group 5, stage IV with bone-dominant metastases and nodal involvement — currently on olaparib 200 mg BID (dose-reduced 3 weeks ago from 300 mg BID), demonstrating continued and deepening biochemical response.
   - PSA of 2.8 ng/mL today represents the best PSA response achieved on olaparib, declining from a baseline of 8.3 ng/mL (66% decline from olaparib start). Importantly, this response has been maintained despite the dose reduction, which is reassuring and biologically plausible — olaparib's mechanism as a PARP inhibitor in BRCA2-deficient tumors may sustain response even at reduced dose. PSA will continue to be trended closely.
   - Testosterone confirmed castrate: ongoing leuprolide castration maintained.
   - Germline BRCA2 (c.5946delT) with confirmed biallelic somatic loss — optimal PARP inhibitor candidate. PROfound data supports continued use.

2. Olaparib-related Grade 3 anemia, now post-dose reduction and transfusion, with Hgb 9.6 g/dL and improving reticulocyte response — suggestive of partial marrow recovery. Grade 3 threshold (Hgb < 8.0 g/dL) has not been re-met. Iron stores fully repleted post-IV ferric carboxymaltose. Grade 1 leukopenia (WBC 4.2 K/uL, ANC 1.9 K/uL) without neutropenia — to be monitored. Mild thrombocytopenia (176 K/uL) stable, not requiring intervention.

3. Bone metastases — multiple sites involving lumbar and thoracic spine, ribs, sternum, bilateral iliac crests, right femoral neck/head, and sacrum. Continued on denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SQ q4 weeks for skeletal-related event prevention. Bone pain minimally symptomatic today (Grade 1, managed with acetaminophen). Calcium and vitamin D supplementation ongoing. Dental check-up should be confirmed to be up to date given prolonged denosumab use (osteonecrosis of the jaw risk counseling reinforced).

4. Hypertension — blood pressure well-controlled at 128/76 on amlodipine 10 mg daily. No changes needed.

5. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled (HbA1c 6.9%) on metformin 500 mg BID. No changes needed. Monitored given prior prednisone use (now discontinued).

6. Mild right upper quadrant tenderness on exam today — new finding; no hepatomegaly identified on physical exam. LFTs reassuringly within normal limits (AST 31, ALT 27, total bili 0.8). May represent referred discomfort from a diaphragmatic process, musculoskeletal etiology, or early/subclinical hepatic involvement. Will be evaluated on upcoming restaging CT. Patient counseled to report worsening or new abdominal symptoms promptly.

---

PLAN

1. OLAPARIB (LYNPARZA) — Continue at 200 mg BID (dose-reduced). The current dose is well-tolerated with improvement in fatigue and resolution of nausea. PSA response is being maintained. No indication to re-escalate to 300 mg at this time given the significant prior hematologic toxicity. Will reassess tolerability at next visit.

2. HEMATOLOGIC MONITORING — CBC with differential to be repeated in 2 weeks (at next visit) to continue monitoring Hgb trajectory. If Hgb declines below 8.0 g/dL again, will consider further dose reduction to 100 mg BID per FDA dosing guidelines, or temporary hold pending hematology guidance. Hematology co-management is active and was engaged at the time of the last transfusion. Iron stores appear repleted — no additional IV iron at this time. No ESA indicated. Thrombocytopenia and leukopenia monitored; ANC currently above thresholds for dose modification.

3. RESTAGING IMAGING — Bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast scheduled in approximately 4-6 weeks (approximately 5 months after olaparib start). This will provide the first formal radiographic response assessment on olaparib. Of note, the new right upper quadrant tenderness today will be specifically evaluated on CT to rule out evolving hepatic metastases. Patient and family informed.

4. LEUPROLIDE — Next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Testosterone will be confirmed castrate at that visit.

5. DENOSUMAB — Last dose 3 weeks ago; continue q4 weeks. Dental hygiene counseling reinforced. Patient reports last dental visit 8 months ago — will refer for dental clearance/check-up given prolonged RANKL inhibitor exposure. Calcium and vitamin D3 supplementation continued.

6. SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT — Acetaminophen 650 mg TID PRN for bone pain; patient instructed to avoid NSAIDs given renal considerations (eGFR 62, borderline CKD 2). Ondansetron 4 mg PRN available for nausea if it recurs.

7. GOALS OF CARE — Patient remains engaged in active cancer-directed therapy and understands the current plan. He is accompanied today by his daughter. Performance status ECOG 1. No formal hospice or palliative care referral at this time; patient is ambulatory and ADL-independent. Psychosocial support offered; patient declines formal social work referral at this time but will reconsider if disease trajectory changes.

8. RETURN TO CLINIC — Follow-up in 2-3 weeks for repeat CBC and clinical assessment. Earlier return or telephone triage if new or worsening symptoms including worsening fatigue, dyspnea, fever, or new bone pain.

9. GENETIC COUNSELING — Family members (adult children) to be referred for genetic counseling given germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (autosomal dominant, 50% risk in first-degree relatives). Patient reports his daughter has been informed; cascade testing discussion ongoing.

Overall, Mr. Kowalski is showing a favorable and durable biochemical response to olaparib at the reduced dose, with the best PSA on this agent to date. Hematologic toxicity remains the primary dose-limiting concern and requires continued close monitoring. Upcoming restaging imaging will be critical for assessing radiographic response. The trajectory is cautiously encouraging at this juncture.

Time spent: 45 minutes (> 50% of visit time spent in counseling and coordination of care).

Dictated and reviewed by: Genitourinary Medical Oncology Attending.
